howItWorks.intro: This emergency generator load calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Running Load = Essential Loads + Motor Loads
Starting Load = Essential Loads + (Motor Load x Starting Multiplier)
Generator kVA = Peak Load / Power Factor / 1000
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: Why does motor starting load matter for generator sizing?
faq.answer: Electric motors draw 3-6x their running current when starting. If the generator cannot handle this surge, it will stall or trip breakers. Always size for the largest motor starting simultaneously with other loads.

faq.question: What power factor should I use?
faq.answer: Use 0.8 for mixed residential loads. Industrial motor loads may be 0.7-0.85. Resistive loads (heaters, lights) are 1.0. Lower power factor means you need a larger generator.

faq.question: Is the Emergency Generator Load Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What essential loads should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate essential loads value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Emergency Generator Load Calculator?
sections.body: The Emergency Generator Load Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your essential loads, largest motor load, motor starting multiplier, you get instant results including total running load, peak starting load, generator size needed. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting total running load right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your essential loads and need to find the right total running load. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Essential Loads (continuous) (Watts)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your essential loads — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: largest motor load, motor starting multiplier, power factor. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your total running load, peak starting load, generator size needed. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your essential loads before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both total running load and peak starting load — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your power factor, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
